cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Andrew Leete was the son of Governor William Leete, who governed both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut — the only person ever to hold both offices. Her first cousin Mary Leete (through the Governor's son William Jr.) also married a Hooker man in the same generation.

bio.notes: Also given as "Mary" — EH gives "Mercy (Mary) Leete"

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Andrew Leete was the son of Governor William Leete, who governed both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut — the only person ever to hold both offices. Her first cousin Mary Leete (through the Governor's son William Jr.) also married a Hooker man in the same generation.

bio.notes: Also given as "Mary" — EH gives "Mercy (Mary) Leete"

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone gives age 98. EH gives age 99.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: He heard his brother died
narrative_blocks.body: On hearing that his younger brother Giles had died at age 96, Samuel Hooker "fell back in his chair and expired" in Kensington on March 1, 1787, age 98. Their sister later wrote: "her brother Samuel died in 1787, ae. 99 — a long lived race." Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife was a granddaughter of the Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mercy Leete, whom he married in 1711, was the granddaughter of Governor William Leete of Connecticut Colony. His uncle James Hooker had also married a Leete granddaughter — Mary Leete, Mercy's first cousin through the same Governor. Two generations of the Hooker family married into the Leete gubernatorial line.

cross_connections.display_label: Her great-grandson Frank exhumed her lost grave in 1898.

narrative_blocks.header: Descendant exhumed her grave in 1898
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Hooker Gridley died at Pompey NY in 1819 and was buried in a small graveyard that was later abandoned. In 1898 her great-great-grandson Frank Gridley Fowler of Bridgeport CT searched out the lost cemetery, located her grave, and had her remains exhumed and reinterred — leaving an account of the search.

death.cause: Killed in action — shot by Native American sharp-shooter at sentry post

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle Samuel Jr enlisted weeks after his June death.

military_service.unit: 10th Company under Capt. Eliphalet Whittlesey of Newington, Col. Phineas Lyman's Regiment
military_service.notes: Enlisted March 8, 1757 at age 22. Killed in action June 10, 1757 while volunteering for a sentry post where multiple soldiers had previously been picked off by Native American sharp-shooters. Served alongside John Beckley (mar 28-Nov 24, 1757) and Silas Beckley (mar 28-Nov 11, 1757, also died). See notable_story 'martin_hooker_sentry_revenge_1757' for the family-tradition sequel preserved in EH 1909.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by Native American sharpshooter at sentry post
narrative_blocks.body: Martin enlisted March 8, 1757 in Capt. Eliphalet Whittlesey's 10th Company under Col. Phineas Lyman's Regiment. He was killed June 10, 1757 while volunteering for a sentry post where multiple soldiers had previously been picked off by Native American sharp-shooters.

narrative_blocks.header: Family tradition: avenged by friend's hog stratagem
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy preserved a tradition that days after Martin's death a close friend volunteered for the same post and shot a hog he saw rooting nearby. Inside the hog skin lay the body of the Native American assassin who had been using it as cover; Martin was reportedly the last sentry killed at that post.

death.cause: Date undocumented; inferred 1777-1785 from widow's remarriage

narrative_blocks.header: Died young; widow remarried within eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Giles married Abigail Smith of Newington in February 1777 at age 31. By February 1785 — eight years later — Abigail had remarried Joseph Woodruff of Farmington, indicating Giles had died as a young man. The exact date and circumstances of his death are not documented in Edward Hooker 1909.

bio.notes: Replacement naming after first Martin (H00410) killed in army June 10, 1757

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his older brother Martin Hooker who was killed at age 19 in the French and Indian War on June 10, 1757 — two years and three months before this Martin was born. Replacement-naming honor.

military_service.notes: Served as Private during the Revolutionary War — unit not documented in EH 1909. WikiTree (https://www.wikitree.com/wiki/Hooker-3392) preserves the Rev War Private record.

narrative_blocks.header: Replacement Martin who became a Revolutionary soldier
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Hooker named this second son Martin in September 1759, two years and three months after the first Martin Hooker was killed at age 19 in the French and Indian War. The replacement Martin lived a long life — Revolutionary War Private, married Hannah Judd of New Britain in 1785, removed to Tinmouth Vermont and afterward to Peru New York. He died at Peru in September 1843 at age 84, two months after Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband Moses lived to 96
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hooker married Moses Gilbert in February 1734 and bore him six children in Berlin, Connecticut. Her son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776, at age 34. Ebenezer's own son Sylvanus later died in the same army.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece by brother Samuel Jr. who married her son Hooker.
Her daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Allyn, dau. of Capt. Thomas Allyn of Windsor and Abigail (dau. of Rev. John Warham).
parents.father_research_notes: Ebenezer Gilbert, merchant of Hartford (d.1726). Will inventoried £4,455 including 4 enslaved persons; served on 1717-1718 school committee. Not added as separate entry.

narrative_blocks.header: He lived to 96
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert was baptized in Hartford in 1707 and died in Berlin, Connecticut in 1803 at approximately 96. His son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. His grandson Sylvanus, son of Ebenezer, enlisted in the same army and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.header: His father's 1726 will inventoried four enslaved people
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert's father Ebenezer Gilbert was a Hartford merchant who died in 1726. His estate of £4,455 included four enslaved people itemized by price: a negro (£100), a negro woman (£45), a boy (£100), a child (£30). He had also served on a 1717-1718 committee that divided Kensington into five school districts — using Gilbert's River as a boundary line.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his son both died
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer Gilbert was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. He was 34. His son Sylvanus, then just 13, grew up to serve in the same army — and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.header: His family built a red brick house around
narrative_blocks.body: The Gilbert family built a red brick house in Berlin, Connecticut, probably around 1709. The bricks were made on the farm itself, and Gilbird's River now washes around into the clay pit. Unusually hard and black, some bricks are used as ornament and 'shine today as if enamelled' — the process by which they were made is now lost.

death.notes: Died unmarried per EH; ~60yr lifespan estimated; born among siblings 1736-1754

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Maternal uncle whose daughter Sarah he married in 1807.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the surname 'Hooker' as his first name in honor of his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert's family. First-name use of the maternal surname is unusual and emphatic — he later married into the Hooker line as well, his second wife Sarah Hooker (H00432) being his maternal first cousin.

military_service.unit: Multiple — Capt. Gad Stanley's Co (1776), Capt. Lankton's Co under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regt (1777), Capt. Matthew Cole's Co (1779)
military_service.notes: Three documented enlistments: (1) June 1776 — four months in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; (2) summer 1777 — three months in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; (3) July 1779 — ten days in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven (Tryon's Raid). Placed on the federal pension list in 1838. DAR Ancestor #A045110. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Revolutionary War enlistments and 1838 pension
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert enlisted three times during the Revolution: four months in June 1776 in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; three months in summer 1777 in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; and ten days in July 1779 in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven during Tryon's Raid. He was placed on the federal pension list in 1838 and is recorded as DAR Ancestor #A045110.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hooker in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert's first wife Candace Sage died May 15, 1805. Two years later on June 30, 1807 he married Sarah Hooker — his maternal first cousin and the daughter of his uncle Samuel Hooker Jr. The marriage united two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker via his daughter Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert and his son Samuel Hooker Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Both spouses died within two days December 1840
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker died December 4, 1840 at Kensington Connecticut at age 77. Hooker Gilbert followed her two days later on December 6, 1840 at age 86 — together they had been married for thirty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker reunited 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert and Sarah Hooker were the marriage point where two branches of the Hooker line came back together. His mother Elizabeth and her father Samuel Jr. had been siblings — both children of Samuel Hooker of Kensington. The cousin marriage produced no documented children of its own but closed a fifty-year gap between the two branches.

bio.notes: Also spelled Jedidiah in some sources

sources.notes: Entry 118 — James Hurlbut. Lists Abigail Hart as first wife, not Mehitable Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Her recorded marriage belonged to another woman
narrative_blocks.body: The 1909 genealogy married her to Dr. James Hurlbut, but the Hurlbut Family Genealogy proves his wife was Abigail Hart — the error stood in the family record for a century. Mehitable’s own life after 1722 remains unrecorded.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut.
South Cemetery, Harwinton, Litchfield County, Connecticut. 41.76470, -73.05970.

bio.notes: Named for mother Sybil Hooker Winchell

narrative_blocks.header: She made the long road west
narrative_blocks.body: Twice married in Connecticut, she and her second husband pulled up and traveled hundreds of miles to Rising Sun, a small town on the Ohio River in Indiana, where she died at eighty — the first of her line to be buried in that state.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut woman who migrated to the Ohio River

cross_connections.display_label: Served in his Troop of horses, Fort William Henry alarm 1757.
Nephew killed June 1757; uncle enlisted that August.
Fellow Hooker descendant in same 1757 Fort William Henry alarm.
Sister Elizabeth's son who married his daughter Sarah 1807.
His son Hooker Gilbert married my daughter Sarah in 1807.

military_service.unit: Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury — Troop of horses
military_service.notes: At age 31 enlisted in Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury's Troop of horses for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757 — 14 days of service. Enlisted approximately two months after his nephew Martin Hooker (H00410) was killed June 10, 1757 in the same war. The connection to Maj. Elizur Talcott (T00459, gen 3 from John Talcott) is a Hooker↔Talcott cross-tree military link.

military_service.unit: Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company, Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment, Col. Mead's drafted men
military_service.notes: Served under Capt. Nathaniel Chapman in Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment at Fishkill, NY. DAR Ancestor #A057686 (Connecticut, Private). Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), p. 522.

narrative_blocks.header: Veteran of two colonial wars
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as Private in two of the colonial era's military campaigns twenty years apart. In August 1757 at age 31 he joined Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for fourteen days during the Fort William Henry alarm. Twenty years later at age 51 he served again from December 1777 to March 1778 in Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company at Fishkill NY — earning DAR ancestor recognition #A057686.

narrative_blocks.header: Enlisted weeks after his nephew Martin's death
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's nephew Martin Hooker — son of Samuel's brother Andrew — was killed in the same war on June 10, 1757 at the deadly sentry post documented in Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy. About two months later in August 1757, Samuel Hooker Jr enlisted at age 31 in Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for the Fort William Henry alarm. Whether the enlistment was motivated by news of Martin's death is undocumented but the timing is suggestive.

cross_connections.display_label: Her son Hooker Gilbert married my daughter Sarah in 1807.
His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-nine with eight children
narrative_blocks.body: Esther was thirty-nine years old when her husband John House IV died in November 1798, leaving her with eight children — the youngest Cyprian still an infant of about eighteen months. She never remarried and lived another fifty years, dying in East Hartford in 1848 at age eighty-nine.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Aunt and mother-in-law via her son's 1807 cousin marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin in late life
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker married Hooker Gilbert on June 30, 1807 in Kensington when she was 44 — her only documented marriage. Hooker Gilbert was her maternal first cousin: her father Samuel Hooker Jr. and his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert were siblings. The couple lived together for thirty-three years without documented children; Sarah died December 4, 1840, and Hooker Gilbert followed two days later.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut. 41.62805, -72.76927

quotes.text: I was born in that part of Farmington which is now Berlin, in Dec. 1752, and have lived in same place to this time.
quotes.attribution: Samuel Cowles, sworn pension affidavit, Berlin Connecticut, September 19, 1832 (U.S. Pension Office)

quotes.text: Samuel Cowles is well known to the Court as a worthy, honest man, a man of truth and a wealthy farmer of Berlin.
quotes.attribution: Probate Judge Thomas Lee of Berlin Connecticut, certifying Samuel's 1832 Revolutionary War pension application

military_service.unit: Capt. Sloper's cavalry company, Col. Bull's Connecticut regiment, Danbury
military_service.notes: Four wartime call-ups documented in his 1832 pension affidavit: (1) July 1776, marched to New York City, ~3 weeks; (2) September 1776, Peekskill and White Plains, ~3 weeks, present during the campaign that lost Fort Washington; (3) October 1777, Peekskill and Red Hook NY, ~2 months under Capt. Sloper or his Lt. Seth Deming; (4) October 1779, New Haven Alarm under Capt. Sloper, ~1 week. Pension rejected under Act of 1832 because total service fell short of the six-month minimum. Served alongside brother Jabez Cowles (H00435).

narrative_blocks.header: Cavalry service through four Connecticut call-ups
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel rode in Capt. Sloper's cavalry company under Col. Bull of Danbury through four wartime call-ups: New York City in summer 1776, Peekskill and White Plains that fall, Red Hook in 1777, and the New Haven Alarm in 1779. His regiment was at White Plains during the campaign that ended with the loss of Fort Washington and 2,800 Continentals captured.

narrative_blocks.header: Served the Revolution with his younger brother Jabez
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served his four wartime call-ups alongside his younger brother Jabez Cowles, both riding under Col. Bull of Danbury. Sixty years later the brothers signed each other's pension paperwork — vouching for a war the federal government had nearly forgotten.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died on the same Kensington parcel
narrative_blocks.body: 'I was born in that part of Farmington which is now Berlin, in Dec. 1752,' Samuel testified in his pension affidavit, 'and have lived in same place to this time.' He died there at eighty-one without ever moving.

narrative_blocks.header: Held the second-row pew, served as fence viewer
narrative_blocks.body: When the Kensington meetinghouse was reseated in 1815, Samuel was assigned the second row — a precise marker of village standing in a colonial congregation. He served the parish as grand juror in 1802 and as fence viewer over several years.

narrative_blocks.header: His pension claim fell short of six months
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel filed a Revolutionary War pension affidavit in 1832 detailing each call-up by date and commanding officer. The Pension Office rejected it because his total service fell short of the six months required under the Act of 1832 — though Probate Judge Thomas Lee certified him to the court as 'a worthy, honest man, a man of truth, and a wealthy farmer of Berlin.'

military_service.unit: Capt. Selah Hart's Co., Col. Erastus Wolcott's regiment (1776 Boston siege); Capt. Sloper's cavalry, Col. Bull's regiment (1776-1779 NY campaigns); Capt. Ichabod Norton's Co. (1778 Albany); Capt. Seth Deming's cavalry (1779 New Haven Alarm)
military_service.notes: Five field tours documented in pension affidavit: (1) Jan 1776 — 3 months at Roxbury MA on Dorchester Hill, Capt. Selah Hart / Col. Wolcott (siege of Boston, occupation after British evacuation Mar 17); (2) Jul 1776 — 3 weeks to Newark NJ, Capt. Sloper / Col. Bull; (3) Oct 1776 — 3 months Peekskill and White Plains NY, same regiment (campaign during which Fort Washington fell); (4) Jan 1778 — 2 weeks Albany NY under Capt. Ichabod Norton, retrieving Gen. Burgoyne's surrendered artillery to Farmington; (5) 1779 — 1 week New Haven Alarm, Capt. Seth Deming, cavalry. PLUS express rider duty for Brig.-Gen. Selah Hart, Nov 7 and Nov 19, 1779, through Simsbury, New Hartford, Norfolk, Torrington, Canaan; paid 45 pounds 13 shillings Dec 20, 1779 (Conn. Archives Rev. War Vol. 16 p. 259). Total field service 7 months 2 weeks. Pensioned 1833. Brother Samuel Cowles (H00434) was witness to his pension application; conversely Jabez witnessed Samuel's 1832 pension application.

quotes.text: Always lived in that part of Berlin, formerly Farmington, where he now lives, and was born in Farmington, Nov. 1755.
quotes.attribution: Jabez Cowles, sworn pension affidavit (paraphrased in U.S. Pension Office record), circa 1832-1833

narrative_blocks.header: On Dorchester Hill the day Boston was lost
narrative_blocks.body: Jabez served three months at Roxbury and on Dorchester Hill in the spring of 1776 under Capt. Selah Hart of Farmington and Col. Erastus Wolcott, occupying the lines that forced the British to evacuate Boston on March 17. He was twenty years old, on his first wartime tour.

narrative_blocks.header: Pension granted 1833 — his brother's was not
narrative_blocks.body: Jabez's federal pension was approved in 1833, recognizing seven months and two weeks of Revolutionary service. His older brother Samuel had filed a year earlier and been rejected for falling short of the six-month minimum, even though both rode the same campaigns under Col. Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home the cannon Burgoyne surrendered at Saratoga
narrative_blocks.body: In January 1778 Jabez marched with Capt. Ichabod Norton to Albany to bring back artillery captured from Gen. Burgoyne after the surrender at Saratoga. The guns were carted south to Farmington across two weeks of winter hauling.

narrative_blocks.header: Five wartime tours plus express rides for Hart
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his five field tours — Boston, Newark, White Plains, Albany, the New Haven Alarm — Jabez rode express for Brig.-Gen. Selah Hart in November 1779, carrying messages through Simsbury, New Hartford, Norfolk, Torrington, and Canaan. He was paid 45 pounds 13 shillings a month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a captain's widow with two stepchildren
narrative_blocks.body: Jabez married Betsey Pratt Scoville in March 1786, the widow of Capt. Daniel Scovil who had died at Colchester five years earlier. She brought two children from her first marriage — Dan and Mary — into the new Kensington household.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas died young
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas died young; the year was not recorded.

parents.father_research_notes: Gov. William Leete, Governor of Connecticut Colony 1676-1683

burial.plot_notes: Interred at the Ancient Burying Ground — same ground as Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: sheltered the regicides at Milford, as Leete did
His granddaughter married the first Guilford Probate Judge.

notable.notable_blurb: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies

narrative_blocks.header: The only man to govern both New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He served Guilford as town clerk from 1639, rose to Governor of New Haven Colony (1661-1664), and then Governor of the Colony of Connecticut (1676-1683) — the only person ever to hold both offices. He is buried at the Ancient Burying Ground in Hartford — the same ground as Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He used the Sabbath and slow horses
narrative_blocks.body: When royal agents arrived demanding the regicides Goffe and Whalley, he read the King's warrant aloud in public — alerting everyone to the agents' purpose — then furnished horses so slowly that the Sabbath intervened before they could depart. By Monday the men were hidden. Roger Newton did the same in Milford; Roger Hooker did the same in Hartford — three men in the same civic network, the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughters married into the Hooker family — two branches
narrative_blocks.body: His son William Leete Jr. fathered Mary Leete, who married Hon. James Hooker of Guilford. His son Andrew Leete fathered Mercy Leete, who married Samuel Hooker of Kensington. Two of his granddaughters independently married into the Hooker family in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Guilford's founding settlers
narrative_blocks.body: Leete left England in 1639 to escape the persecution of Puritans he had witnessed as a court clerk. He was among the twenty-five who signed the plantation covenant at sea and helped found Guilford, becoming one of the seven pillars of its church.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept Guilford's records for over twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Leete served as town clerk of Guilford from 1639 until 1662, alongside terms as magistrate and deputy. The long climb through colony offices carried him eventually to the governorship.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. John Payne of Southoe, Huntingdonshire.

bio.bio_blurb: First wife of Gov. William Leete; mother of his ten children

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of all ten Leete children
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Payne, daughter of Rev. John Payne of Southoe, married William Leete in England in 1636 and crossed to Guilford with him. She bore all ten of his children and died in 1668, before he rose to the Connecticut governorship.

crossConnections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.
